page.title: Git & Version Control Basics
page.description: Learn the essentials of Git, repositories, staging areas, committing, and branching.

sections.title: What is Git?
sections.content: Git is a **distributed version control system (DVCS)** designed to handle everything from small to very large projects with speed and efficiency. Unlike older centralized systems (like SVN), every Git clone is a full-fledged repository with complete history and full version-tracking capabilities, independent of network access or a central server.

tabs.label: Working Directory
tabs.content: The **Working Directory** (or Working Tree) consists of the actual files you are editing on your computer's filesystem. Changes here are unstaged and untracked until you add them.

tabs.label: Staging Area
tabs.content: The **Staging Area** (also known as the Index) is a pre-commit holding area. It tracks the exact modifications that will be packaged into the next commit. This allows you to selectively group changes.

tabs.label: Git Repository
tabs.content: The **Git Repository** (the `.git` directory) is where Git permanently stores your metadata and object database. Commits saved here are part of the secure, immutable project history.

items.title: Modify files
items.description: Edit files in your working directory.

questions.question: Which command moves changes from the Working Directory to the Staging Area?
questions.explanation: The command `git add` is used to stage your changes, preparing them for the next commit.
optionExplanations: git commit saves staged changes to the repository database.
git push uploads local commits to a remote server.
Correct! git add stages the specified file changes.
git checkout is used to switch branches or restore files.

questions.question: Which of the following describes Git's model of version control?
questions.explanation: Git is a Distributed Version Control System (DVCS) because every repository holds the full history and copies of the files.
optionExplanations: Centralized systems keep the history on a single remote server.
Git supports remote synchronization, so it is not local-only.

questions.question: What is the primary role of the Staging Area (Index) in Git?
options: To store backup zip archives of the codebase
To upload changes to GitHub immediately
To act as an intermediate stage where commits are crafted before being finalized
To run linting and testing scripts automatically
questions.explanation: The Staging Area lets you choose exactly which modifications to package into the next commit, providing a transition step between editing and saving.
optionExplanations: Git does not use zip archives for staging.
Uploading to GitHub is done using git push.
Correct! It allows selective staging of changes.
Staging itself does not run tests, though pre-commit hooks can.

sentences.text: To download files and history from a remote repository for the first time, use the command: git ___ <url>
To verify the current state of files (untracked, modified, or staged), run the command: git ___

pairs.right: Create a new local Git repository
